去年，在美国商务部禁止华为进入其美国供应链后，这家中国电话和网络设备制造商争先恐后地寻找新的来源。它最怀念的一个美国供应商是谷歌，因为禁令阻止了谷歌向华为交付谷歌移动服务版本的Android。虽然这在中国无关紧要，但无法为其国际车型配备谷歌生态系统，可能会让该制造商损失一些销售额。在中国，谷歌的大部分Android应用都被禁止了。
2019年8月，华为宣布开发了一款名为Harmony的新操作系统。许多人认为，华为将立即使用这一新操作系统来取代Android。事实证明并非如此。华为消费者集团首席执行官余承东(Richard Yu)指出，Harmony基于微内核，可以在智能扬声器、平板电脑、智能手表、电脑、汽车和智能手机等广泛的设备上使用。华为还指出，HarmonyOS将在配备少量RAM和需要数百GB内存的设备上运行。到目前为止，Harmony OS只在电视机上提供给消费者。
据MyFixGuide报道，余承东今天表示，华为将在2021年出货其首款搭载HarmonyOS操作系统的手机。这位长期担任华为高管的高管宣布，该公司计划于9月10日(周四)在2020年HDC开发者大会期间推出第二版Harmony OS。几款搭载该操作系统的新设备将很快发布，今年华为表示，其新款智能手表将由HarmonyOS驱动。
俞敏洪承认，华为一直坐在运行HarmonyOS的手机上，但由于与谷歌有协议，该公司不得不等待。但这并不意味着该公司在未来对该平台不抱很高的期望。这位高管表示，HarmonyOS最终将成为一个全球性的平台。
在之前的一次演讲中，俞敏洪表示，即将推出的华为Mate 40系列将采用新的5 nm麒麟芯片系列麒麟9000。这些组件不仅将使用新的5 nm工艺生产，而且还将拥有更强大的5G和AI功能、CPU和GPU。但美国实施的一项新出口规定禁止代工厂向华为运送使用美国技术制造的芯片。俞敏洪指出，不幸的是，在美国的制裁下，台积电只接受9月15日之前的订单。到9月15日，它将无法为华为生产芯片。因此麒麟9000可能是我们最后一代华为麒麟高端芯片。
去年，华为是台积电的第二大客户，仅次于苹果，但9月14日之后，这家全球最大的个体晶圆代工企业将不被允许向台积电发货。据报道，在此之前，台积电一直在全天24小时运行其装配线，生产尽可能多的芯片，直到不允许向华为发货为止。中国最大的晶圆代工企业中芯国际(SMIC)不是一个可行的替代品，因为它是台积电后面的几个工艺节点。据报道，芯片设计公司联发科一直在寻求为华为开发尖端芯片的许可。该公司似乎不太可能获得美国的许可，特别是考虑到联发科依赖台积电生产芯片。此外，美国政府官员抱怨华为绕过实体名单禁令的能力。
假设华为已经有能力储备5 nm芯片，该公司有一点时间-注意你没有太多-找到5 nm SoC的新来源。
note_en:
Last year, after the U.S. Commerce Department banned Huawei from its U.S. supply chain, the Chinese phone and networking equipment manufacturer scrambled to find new sources. The one stateside supplier that it missed the most was Google since the ban prevented the latter from delivering the Google Mobile Services version of Android to Huawei. While it didn't matter in China where most of Google's Android apps are banned, not being able to equip its international models with the Google ecosystem might have cost the manufacturer some sales.
   In August of 2019,  Huawei announced that it had  developed a new operating system called Harmony. Many assumed that this new operating system would be immediately used by Huawei to replace Android. This turned out not to be the case. Richard Yu, CEO of Huawei's consumer group, pointed out that  Harmony is based on a microkernel allowing it to be used on a wide range of devices such as smart speakers, tablets, smartwatches, computers, autos, and smartphones. Huawei also noted that HarmonyOS will work on devices equipped with a small amount of RAM and those requiring hundreds of gigabytes of memory. Thus far, Harmony OS has been available for consumers only on television sets.
    According to MyFixGuide, Mr. Yu said today that  in 2021, Huawei will ship its first handset powered by HarmonyOS. What set off this announcement by the long-time Huawei executive was word that the company plans on introducing a second version of Harmony OS this coming Thursday, September 10th, during the 2020 HDC Developers Conference. Several new devices powered by the operating system will soon be announced and this year Huawei says that its new smartwatches will be driven by HarmonyOS.
  Yu did admit that Huawei has been sitting on a phone running HarmonyOS, but the company has had to wait because of an agreement it has with Google. But that doesn't mean that the company doesn't have high hopes for the platform in the future. The executive says that HarmonyOS will eventually become a worldwide platform.
  In a previous speech, Yu said that the upcoming Huawei Mate 40 series will be powered by a new series of 5nm Kirin chips, the Kirin 9000. Not only will these components be produced using the new 5nm process, but they will also have more powerful 5G and AI capabilities, CPUs, and GPUs. But a new export rule put into place by the U.S. prevents foundries from shipping chips to Huawei that were made using U.S. technology. Yu notes, "Unfortunately, under the sanctions of the United States, TSMC only accepted orders before September 15th. By September 15th, it will not be able to produce chips for Huawei. So Kirin 9000 may be our last generation of Huawei Kirin high-end chips."
  Huawei was TSMC's second-largest customer after Apple last year but the world's largest individual foundry will not be allowed to ship to the Chinese manufacturer after September 14th. Until that date, TSMC is reportedly running its assembly line 24 hours a day to produce as many as chips that it can until it isn't allowed to ship to Huawei. China's largest foundry, SMIC, is not a viable replacement since it is several process nodes behind TSMC. Chip designer Mediatek has reportedly been seeking permission to develop cutting- edge chips for Huawei. It would seem unlikely for the company to get that permission from the U.S., especially considering that Mediatek relies on TSMC to produce its chips. Additionally, administration officials in the U.S. have complained about Huawei's ability to skirt the Entity List ban.
  Assuming that Huawei has been able to stock up on 5nm chips, the company has a little time-not much mind you-to find a new source of 5nm SoCs.
title: 华为称明年将发布HarmonyOS手机
title_en: Huawei says that it will release a HarmonyOS phone next year

note:
上周末，Netflix悄悄推出了五年来首部查理·考夫曼(Charlie Kaufman)电影：“我在思考终结一切”(I&#m Think of End Things)，沉浸在围绕上映的两部绝对成功的大片的炒作中，这两部大片对目前的电影现状有着截然不同的态度。考夫曼神秘而荒谬的世界--从改编电影到“当约翰·马尔科维奇”(John Malkovich)，再到“无影无踪的心灵的永恒阳光”--往往取决于宏大的概念和更宏大的想法。因此，虽然这位导演的作品可能没有特尼特和花木兰那么有观众潜力，但它确实能让你印象深刻(比如，“永恒的阳光”让你登上了有史以来最好的科幻电影排行榜)。对于一部分影迷来说，这部电影绝对是2020年劳动节周末最受期待的电影。
考夫曼最终选择与一家主要的流媒体服务合作，这一事实意味着我正在考虑结束一切，理论上也有可能帮助这位电影制作人找到最多的观众。但是，嗯..。虽然我想要结束这一切总是有些离奇，但其中最令人困惑的可能是它被称为一部Netflix电影。准备好在这个平台上大放异彩吧，这是迄今为止最奇怪的一部电影。
量子物理学生露西(杰西·巴克利饰)和她的男友杰克(杰西·普莱蒙斯饰)出发前往俄克拉何马州的乡村农田，这样她就可以第一次见到他的父母。两人约会的时间不长，但露西内心的想法已经表明他们不会再约会很久了。我应该很兴奋，期待着许多人中的第一个-但我不是，露西讲述了他们幽闭恐惧症的早期驾车经历。当他们最终到达的时候？&#34；万物必死，这是事实…。这是人类独有的幻想，认为事情会变得更好，也许是源于人类独特的理解，即它们不会变得更好。“。
不过，露西仍然认为杰克很好。他对她很好。他有兴趣，也有提高的动力。显然，家庭对他来说很重要，因为他将为这顿晚餐驾驭看起来像是一场正在形成的暴风雪。露西需要今晚回家才能赶得上早上上班，杰克最初也承诺了这个紧凑的时间表。
不过，暴风雪很快就成了露西最不担心的事情。杰克的父母(托尼·科莱特和大卫·休利斯饰)几乎立刻给人一种奇怪的感觉。无伤大雅，基于第一印象，但完全奇怪。当甜点端上来的时候，露西感觉到有什么不对劲。她不断接到露西打来的未接电话。这只家里的狗不停地甩掉不存在的水。墙上挂着的杰克宝宝的照片断断续续地看起来很像露西宝宝。杰克警告她说，这座老农舍没什么特别之处，尤其是楼上不起眼的卧室和下面未完工的地下室。但随着暴风雪的持续恶化，杰克慢慢失去了迅速返回的热情，露西开始探索她认为是农舍的地方，但这个地方似乎越来越像是某个固定的地方，对时间和现实的定义非常不稳定。
我正在考虑结束这件事，我如此努力地陷入抽象之中，以至于你不禁想知道，即使在你观看的过程中，全景是什么。通常，这是考夫曼电影魅力的一部分，但这是一个危险的命题，因为有些东西是Netflix独有的。在隔离前，一旦你买了一张戏票，你就会全神贯注，只专注于屏幕。但今天的流媒体电影不得不一次又一次地争夺你的注意力，因为你和其他人呆在家里，你的手机伸手可及。在两个多小时的时间里，我正在考虑结束这些事情，这将考验许多观众不止几次的承诺。
这个项目从一开始就是为Netflix设想的，所以它的最终版本给人的感觉是如此不像Netflix，这是令人惊讶的。是的，这家流媒体服务公司还有其他真正放纵的电影制作(罗马人)和蜿蜒、有条不紊的故事(《爱尔兰人》)，但这些电影是例外。Netflix更经常擅长各种流派的精简、快速发展的电影，从《摘录》到《我以前喜欢的所有男孩》，再到《多莱米蒂是我的名字》(Dolemite Is My Name)。相比之下，我想结束一切的前半个小时完全发生在一辆灯光昏暗的汽车里，露西和杰克在近距离拍摄。当露西和杰克到达农舍时，事情确实变得越来越复杂，但即便如此，这也是转瞬即逝的。很快，杰克和露西就逃回了他们的车里，在那里，关于电影制片人约翰·卡萨维茨1974年由大卫·福斯特·华莱士(David Foster Wallace)留下的遗产执导的惊悚片“影响力下的女人”(A Women Under The Influence)以及个性本质的漫长讨论很快就会到来。“你在故意迟钝，”露西对杰克说(我想是对考夫曼不是)。
总体而言，我考虑的结局是非常戏剧性的：最小的布景(汽车、农舍、奶制品皇后的翻版和一所学校)，动作很少的长场戏和非常深入的对话，a
考夫曼的职业生涯建立在超现实主义电影上，而我想要结束一切的想法和他之前的作品一样多，也同样无视自然规律-它只是没有把一切都弄清楚。有时，我发现自己在想，我们是否在与痴呆症作斗争：例如，杰克在谈话中打电话给露西·路易莎(Lucy&34；Louisa)和露西亚(Lucia)，没有人会跳过一拍；我们还看到不同年龄的人出现在同一个夜晚。或者，也许这个农场体现了这样一种想法，即你的整个生命在你死前闪现在你面前，就像通过露西我们似乎看到了杰克的存在。配角，从杰克的母亲到在奶制品皇后仿制店做奶昔的年轻女子，似乎都打破了第四面墙，警告露西未来会遇到一些麻烦，这个标题可以用更致命的光来解读，露西内心的独白很多地讲述了存在的本质：“我们是静止的，时间通过我们…流逝。”留下我们，我不知道，已经死了，“她一度说。“可悲的是，很少有人在死前拥有自己的灵魂，”她在另一位读者面前沉思。
如果我正在考虑结束这件事，并最终想要说一些关于感情的事情，那么这件事肯定是没有希望的。考夫曼的代表作“永恒的阳光”围绕着同样的主题，认为坠入爱河的旅程是值得穿越的，即使最终目的地更多的是悲剧结局，而不是悲剧结局。但十多年后，考夫曼有了一个非常不同的曲调。露西和杰克在这个越来越低迷的时刻艰难前行，杰克没有表现出任何救赎的品质。他的行为就像一张经常纠正露西的活生生的传票，他没有意识到露西越来越迫切地要求回家，他也不愿意费心把他们的关系起源的细节说得清清楚楚。如果两人的关系注定是这样的，露西的挣扎建议你根本不要上车。
我正在考虑结束一些事情，很可能会成为一部思考、阅读或讨论起来比看起来更有趣的电影-再一次，这让人觉得Netflix到目前为止拥有的其他一切都有点违反直觉。“机器人先生”的创作者萨姆·埃斯梅尔称这是他今年最喜欢的电影，同时在推特上指出它，嗯，缺乏情节。我个人很快就去争先恐后地寻找这本书的摘要，这本书的灵感来自伊恩·里德(Ian Reid)2016年的首部同名小说。这也许会让我看到的东西更适合一个想法；当然，露西的一些名言听起来不同：大多数人都是其他人：他们的想法是别人的观点，他们的生活是模仿的，他们的激情是一句引语。(注：大多数人都是其他人：他们的想法是别人的观点，他们的生活是模仿的，他们的激情是一句引语。)。
所有这些都假设考夫曼想要保持对原始故事的忠诚，这是不能保证的。甚至在我们知道影院不会是一个选择之前，这位导演就打算避开现在与流媒体格式相关的许多特点。我正在考虑结束一些事情，很可能会在外面有咖啡馆等待观众的艺术馆电影院里放映得更好，但就像露西的最终命运一样，我们永远不会知道。
note_en:
Lost in all the hype around the release of two surefire blockbusters with  very  different approaches to the current state of cinema, this weekend Netflix quietly debuted the first Charlie Kaufman film in five years:  I'm Thinking of Ending Things. Kaufman's enigmatic and absurd worlds—from  Adaptation to  Being John Malkovich to  Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind—often hinge on big concepts and even bigger ideas. So while the director's work may not have the same audience potential as  Tenet or  Mulan, it can really stick with you ( Eternal Sunshine made Ars' list of  the best sci-fi  ever, for instance). To a subset of film fans,  this is definitely the most anticipated film of Labor Day weekend 2020.
 The fact that Kaufman finally opted to partner with a major streaming service means  I'm Thinking of Ending Things theoretically has the potential to help the filmmaker find his biggest audience, too. But, well... while  I'm Thinking of Ending Things was always going to be somewhat bizarre, the most perplexing thing about it may be calling it a "Netflix film." Prepare to hit play on the platform's oddest release to date.
  Quantum physics student Lucy (Jessie Buckley) and her boyfriend Jake (Jesse Plemons) set out for rural Oklahoma farmland so she can meet his parents for the first time. The two haven't been dating very long, but Lucy's inner thoughts already suggest they won't be dating much longer. "I should be excited, looking towards the first of many—but I'm not," Lucy narrates early in their claustrophobic car ride. When they finally arrive? "Everything has to die, that’s the truth... it’s a uniquely human fantasy that things will get better, born, perhaps, out of a uniquely human understanding that they will not.”
 But still, Lucy sees Jake as  fine. He treats her nicely. He has interests and a drive to improve. And evidently family matters to him, because he'll navigate what looks like a developing blizzard for this dinner. Lucy needs to get home tonight to make it to work in the morning, and Jake initially commits to that tight timeline, too.
 The blizzard quickly becomes the least of Lucy's concerns, though. Jake's parents (Toni Collette and David Thewlis) almost immediately come across as strange. Harmless, based on first impressions, but totally strange. And by the time dessert has been brought out, Lucy senses something's up. She keeps getting missed calls from "Lucy." The family dog won't stop shaking off non-existent water. And pictures of baby Jake on the wall intermittently look a whole awful lot like baby Lucy. Jake warned her the old farmhouse didn't have much to it, particularly its nondescript bedrooms upstairs and the unfinished basement beneath. But as the blizzard keeps worsening and Jake slowly loses his enthusiasm for heading back promptly, Lucy starts exploring what she believed to be a farmhouse but what increasingly seems to be some fixed place with very fluid definitions of time and reality.
     I'm Thinking of Ending Things leans so hard into the abstract that you can't help but wonder what the full picture is even while your viewing is in progress. Normally, that's part of the charm with a Kaufman film, but it's a dangerous proposition with something exclusive to Netflix. In the  pre-quarantimes, once you purchased a ticket for the theater, you were  pot committed and focused only on the screen. But a streaming movie today has to compete for your attention again and again as you're stuck at home with others and your phone is within arm's reach.  I'm Thinking of Ending Things will test many viewers' commitment more than a few times over its two-hours-plus.
 This project was  envisioned for Netflix from the start, so it's surprising how un-Netflix the final version feels. Yes, the streaming service has other examples of truly indulgent filmmaking ( Roma) and meandering, methodically paced stories ( The Irishman), but those films are the exceptions. Netflix more regularly excels at lean, fast-moving films across genres, from   Extraction to  To All the Boys I Loved Before to  Dolemite Is My Name. By contrast, the first half hour of  I'm Thinking of Ending Things takes place entirely in a dimly lit car with Lucy and Jake in close up. Things truly ratchet up when Lucy and Jake reach the farmhouse, but even that is fleeting. Soon Jake and Lucy escape back to their car, where lengthy discussions of filmmaker John Cassavetes’s 1974 thriller   A Woman Under the Influence, David Foster Wallace's legacy, and the nature of personality soon follow. “You’re being willfully obtuse,” Lucy says to Jake (and not to Kaufman, I think).
 Overall,  I'm Thinking of Ending Things comes off very stage play-y: minimal sets (the car, the farmhouse, a Dairy Queen knock-off, and a school), long scenes made of very little action and very in-depth dialogue, and lots of quotes, ideas, or moments that feel allegorical as they're happening. (This surface-level analogy gets amplified by a few spoiler-y things, too.) The performances can also have their volume turned all the way up quite often. Thewlis and Collette in particular clearly had green lights to lean into their characters' weirdness as much as they pleased, and Plemons' Jake fluctuates between feeling like an illusion and a newish-but-uninspiring boyfriend. I had more than a few flashbacks to parsing  Waiting for Godot in high school English class: in  I'm Thinking of Ending Things, eventually you realize what's being grappled with in script was always meant to be more important than what's happening in front of your eyes.
 Kaufman has made his career on surrealist cinema, and  I'm Thinking of Ending Things has just as many ideas and just as much disregard for the laws of nature as his previous works—it just doesn't land everything as cleanly. At times, I found myself wondering if we were in the mind of someone battling with dementia: Jake, for instance, calls Lucy "Louisa" and "Lucia" in conversation and no one skips a beat; we also see people who appear at different ages within the same "night." Or, maybe this farm is a manifestation of the idea that your entire life flashes before you right before death, as through Lucy we seem to see Jake's existence stuck in fast forward. Supporting characters from Jake's mom to the young woman making milkshakes at a Dairy Queen knock-off all seem to break the fourth wall to warn Lucy of some trouble ahead, the title could be interpreted in a more fatal light, and Lucy's inner monologue homes in on the nature of existence  a lot: “We’re stationary, time passes through us… leaving us, I don’t know, dead,” she says at one point. “It’s tragic how few people possess their souls before they die,” she ponders at another.
 And if  I'm Thinking of Ending Things ultimately wants to say something about relationships, that something definitely isn't hopeful.  Eternal Sunshine, Kaufman's masterpiece, centers on the same topic and argues the journey of falling in love is worth traversing through even if the ultimate destination is a tragic ending more often that not. But more than a decade later, Kaufman has a much different tune to play. Lucy trudges forward with Jake in this increasingly down spiraling moment without Jake showcasing any redeeming qualities. He acts like a walking citation correcting Lucy with regularity, he fails to recognize her increasingly urgent pleas to get home, and he can't be bothered to keep details of their relationship origins straight. If relationships are destined to be like this, Lucy's struggle suggests don't get in the car at all.
 I'm Thinking of Ending Things will probably become a film that's more fun to think through, read about, or discuss than it is to watch—which, again, feels a little counterintuitive to everything else Netflix has up to this point.   Mr. Robot creator Sam Esmail  called it his favorite film of the year while simultaneously noting its, um, lack of plot on Twitter. Personally, I quickly went scrambling for a summary of the book that inspired this,  Ian Reid's 2016 debut novel of the same name. That maybe made what I saw "fit" into an idea a bit better; certainly some of Lucy's quotes ring differently: "Most people are other people: their thoughts are someone else’s opinion, their lives are mimicry, their passions a quotation."
 All that assumes Kaufman wanted to remain loyal to the original story, which is not guaranteed. Even before we knew theaters wouldn't be an option, this auteur intended to eschew a lot of the characteristics now associated with the streaming format.  I'm Thinking of Ending Things would likely play better in an art house cinema with a cafe waiting for audiences right outside, but much like Lucy's ultimate fate, we will never know.
title: 我在考虑结束：迄今为止最不像Netflix的“Netflix电影”
title_en: I’m Thinking of Ending Things: The most un-Netflix “Netflix film” to date

note:
更新，9月。2020年7月7日：这是美国的劳动节周末，尽管我们中的大多数人现在也把办公室称为家，但Ars的员工正在度过一个长周末来休息和放松。8月底标志着卡特里娜飓风在路易斯安那州登陆15周年，联邦堤坝坍塌，新奥尔良市永远改变了。我们计划重新铺设几件来自档案馆的物品，以便在这个假期期间保持灯光亮着，所以我们重新铺设了这张照片，看看NASA是如何在新奥尔良郊外的米丘德组装设施成功经受住卡特里娜飓风的影响的。这篇报道最初发表于2015年8月，下面似乎没有变化。
路易斯安那州米丘德-2005年8月29日，卡特里娜飓风来袭，联邦堤坝坍塌，新奥尔良大都市区接踵而至的混乱局面接踵而至。
到目前为止，损害已经有了很好的记录。如此多的人流离失所，以至于十年后新奥尔良仍然只占风暴前人口的80%左右。超过1200人死亡-这是自1928年以来美国暴风雨中死亡人数最多的一次。该市80%的地区被洪水淹没，造成财产损失，据国家飓风中心估计，损失达1080亿美元。几乎不考虑标准，卡特里娜飓风是有史以来袭击美国的最具破坏性的大西洋风暴。
伍德住在大约一个小时车程外的密西西比州皮卡尤恩，幸运的是，他的其他家人有办法和途径前往北部的哈蒂斯堡避难。但与大多数住在密西西比三角洲或路易斯安那州南部时在大新奥尔良工作的人不同，伍德的公司拒绝在本世纪风暴前夕关闭-尽管新奥尔良有史以来第一次强制疏散。不可能。首先，数十亿之前和未来的工作都岌岌可危。伍德的直属同事-2000多名同事-的生计也是如此。见鬼，伍德参与的整个全国行动可能悬而未决，这取决于他的设施是否能幸存下来，他的设施位于下9区以东仅15英里处。
因此，伍德，一个高大能干的人，已经在同一地点工作了20多年，开始做分配给他的工作。面对横跨400英里的风暴前缘和每小时120英里以上的大风的直接影响，他是一个38人团队的一员，他们不得不在现场安然度过卡特里娜飓风，以保卫公司832英亩的毗邻水域的设施。目标是什么？尽可能多地保持其完好无损，并保持在线。
这项任务令人望而生畏--“我们从气象站得知，它将比以前的暴风雨更糟糕，”伍德说。“这看起来像是一场完美的风暴”--但赌注简直就是天方夜谭。因此，伍德长途跋涉了大约40英里，来到路易斯安那州的小米丘德，准备在320号楼过夜。这个不起眼的办公空间坐落在NASA的米丘德组装设施的后面，该组织的燃料箱自20世纪60年代以来就是在这里制造的。
这将是伍德和他的公司连续大约30个晚上在米丘德庭院度过的第一个晚上。
正如你可能预料到的那样，考虑到其庞大的东南部美国特遣队，美国国家航空航天局(NASA)已经制定了缓解风暴的计划。特别是米胡德，考虑到它的地理位置，在卡特里娜飓风之前的时间里，它曾经面临过25到30次这样的事件。正如伍德解释的那样，渡过难关的船员是典型的风暴前和风暴后过程的一部分。在他们的职责中，一名顺风车船员巡视设施，以确定任何可能的易受破坏的区域，捆绑任何可能被证明是危险的材料，在现场维护补给和发电机，并最终帮助导航，无论后果带来什么，以便让设施恢复正常。如果暴风雨看起来足够糟糕(卡特里娜飓风也合格了)，顺风车机组人员也将是现场唯一的小组，这是抵御气候变化的最后一道防线。伍德告诉Ars：“我们在这里经历了无数的风暴，但通常是两天、三天，你就可以恢复运转了。”“这次有很大不同。”
伍德声称，风暴过后10年，一些记忆已经消失，但他还记得最初24小时的大部分感觉。雨是从二十八号夜间开始下的。风太大了，很快你就站在320号楼外面，看不清任何通常可以看到的校园--包括450号楼，这座非常重要的南瓜屋位于设施的最南端，靠近当时17英尺高的堤坝。伍德记得，为了保持一种平静的感觉，她只是简单地回到了超聚焦状态，变得“专注于某事”。
伍德说：“泵房下面有一点灯光，所以只要我看到那盏灯，我就知道水泵在运转。”“我知道他们抽水只是为了挡雨。我们不知道我们是否被洪水淹没了，但如果你站在这座建筑(320)的前面，我们就会看到水位上升的地方。如果它没有击中这里的第一步，我们就没问题。“。
起初，伍德的一些搭便车同事驻扎在南瓜房里。他们监测了里面的卡特彼勒水泵(四个设备，每分钟可以处理6.2万加仑的水)是否可以防止不断上升的水位超过堤坝，淹没几百码外320号楼附近的制造区。但美国国家航空航天局(NASA)的协议考虑到了即使是最勇敢的乘员的安全。一旦风力达到一定的强风，所有人都必须被带到一个安全的区域(在这种情况下，是320号楼)，并保持封锁状态，直到危险平息。在卡特里娜飓风期间，这个临界点出现在凌晨3点。
伍德回忆说：“我们通常不会放弃南瓜房，但我们不得不在半夜去把它们带回来。”“所以在清晨，两个人开了一辆翻斗车，那里非常荒凉--你看不见车道，最上面是一片漆黑。”卡特里娜飓风大约是我在这里工作的这些年里，我能记得的第一次，我们的网站断电了。我的意思是，这座城市断电了--这是独一无二的。“。
从那时起，伍德相信这真的是“一触即发”。基于之前的风暴，他相信只要大自然给他们机会，搭便车团队就能让设施重新运行。但即使在那一刻，卡特里娜飓风的破坏性潜力也是令人痛苦地显而易见的，搭乘人员也很清楚其后果。那是在2005年，哥伦比亚号悲剧仅仅发生在两年前，米丘德预计将改装一些外部坦克，作为返回太空任务的一部分。虽然每个人都知道太空计划将在未来十年的某个时候结束，但失去米丘德将极大地影响这一时间框架。
“如果我们失去了堤坝，我们就会关闭NASA的太空计划，”伍德说。“我们这里的每一辆车都是我们制造的，所以除非你经过新奥尔良，否则你怎么去太空呢？”这是你经历过的最灾难性的事件。如果米胡德被完全淹没，美国国家航空航天局(NASA)不得不说，‘好吧，我们现在退出太空业务了。’那将是一年又一年的损害。“。
伍德当时是设施主管，在他看来，排水从来不是问题。这些设施的排水系统可以容纳一定数量的水，如果给予一定的时间，这些水最终会流出。但是，如果水泵在水还在来的时候就停了，那么这种计算就会突然悲剧性地失去平衡。
所以那天晚上，团队不得不做出决定。改变水泵的速度是可能的，但它们是水冷装置，太用力就会有过热和故障的风险。最终，伍德和他的公司选择了推动油门-它成功了。
伍德说：“我从来没有想过会有风险，但看着下雨的方式，你可以看看道路，知道你永远不会用泵抽水。”“我们的计算大约是10亿加仑的水被冲走，所以我们让水泵保持运转，因为总会有某种渗漏回来。”
第二天早上，米丘德出行团队得知他们已经完成了主要任务：设施不在水下。然而，这似乎是老真蒂利路上唯一不是的东西-米丘德的主要制造业拖累。
“我们直到第二天早上(8/30)才知道我们基本上是一个孤岛，”伍德说。“我们被水包围了。在晚上和第二天早上，我们知道那里有很多雨和风，但你永远想不到你会被水包围。我们保持我们的水泵运转，做了正确的事情，我们被训练要做的事情。第二天和30天后的灾难性事件，就是你看到人们做不寻常事情的地方。“

Update, Sept. 7, 2020: It's Labor Day Weekend in the US, and even though most of us now also call home "the office," Ars staff is taking a long weekend to rest and relax. The end of August marked 15 years since Hurricane Katrina made landfall in Louisiana, the federal levees failed, and the city of New Orleans changed forever. We planned on resurfacing a few pieces from the archives to keep the lights on over this holiday, so we're resurfacing this look at how NASA managed to weather the impact of Katrina at its Michoud Assembly Facility just outside New Orleans. This story originally ran in August 2015 and it appears unchanged below.
  MICHOUD, La.—On August 29, 2005, Hurricane Katrina came, the federal levees failed, and chaos ensued in the New Orleans metro area.
 By now the damage is  well documented. So many people were displaced that New Orleans still only sits at approximately  80 percent of its pre-storm population a decade later. More than 1,200 people died—the most for a US storm since 1928. And 80 percent of the city flooded, causing property damage since estimated at $108 billion by the National Hurricane Center. Almost regardless of metric, Katrina stands as the most devastating Atlantic storm to ever hit the US.
  Wood lived roughly an hour away in Picayune, Mississippi, and luckily the rest of his family had the means and access to get north to Hattiesburg for safety. But unlike most folks working in Greater New Orleans while living in the Mississippi Delta or Southern Louisiana, Wood’s company refused to shut down on the eve of the storm of the century—despite New Orleans' first-ever mandatory evacuation. It couldn’t. For starters,  billions in prior and future work were on the line. The livelihood of Wood’s direct coworkers—more than 2,000 colleagues—was too. Heck, the entire national operation that Wood was a part of likely hung in the balance depending on whether his facility, just 15 miles east of the Lower 9th Ward, could survive.
 So Wood, a large and capable man who’d already logged 20-plus years of employment at the same location, set out to do the job he was assigned. Facing direct impact from a 400-miles-across stormfront and 120+ mph winds, he was part of a 38-person team that had to ride out Hurricane Katrina  on site to defend the company’s 832-acre water adjacent facility. The goal? Keep as much of it intact and online as possible.
 This task was daunting—“We knew from the weather station it was going to be worse than previous storms,” Wood says. “It looked like the perfect storm”—but the stakes were literally out of this world. So Wood traveled the roughly 40 miles down to tiny Michoud, Louisiana, and prepared to spend the night at Building 320. The unassuming office space sits toward the back of  NASA’s Michoud Assembly Facility, where the organization's fuel tanks have been made since the 1960s.
 It’d be the first night of roughly 30 straight that Wood and company would spend on the Michoud grounds.
  As you might expect given its large Southeastern US contingent, NASA has  plans in place for storm mitigation. Michoud in particular, given its location, had faced 25 to 30 such events in its time before Katrina. As Wood explained, ride out crews are part of the typical pre- and post- storm processes. Among their duties, a ride out crew tours facilities to identify any possible areas susceptible to damage, ties down any materials that could prove dangerous if blown about, maintains provisions and generators on site, and eventually helps navigate whatever the aftermath brings in order to get the facility back online. If a storm looks bad enough (and Katrina qualified), the ride out crew will also be the only group on site, a last line of defense against the elements. “We’ve had numerous storms we’ve been here for and gone through, but usually it’s two days, three days and you’re back up and running,” Wood told Ars. “This was so much different.”
 Wood claims some of the memories are gone 10 years after the storm, but he can recall much of what that initial 24 hours felt like. The rains began overnight on the 28th. It came so heavily, with winds so loud, that soon you couldn’t stand outside Building 320 and make out any of the normally visible campus—including Building 450, the all-important pumphouse at the very southern end of the facilities near a then 17-foot levee. To maintain a sense of calm, Wood remembers simply reverting to hyperfocus, becoming “fixated on something.”
 “There’s a little light down at the pump house, so as long as I saw that light, I knew the pump was running,” Wood said. “I knew they were pumping water just to keep the rain out. We didn’t know if we’d been flooded, but if you stand in front of this building (320), this would be where we’d see the water rise. If it didn’t hit the first step here, we were OK.”
    Initially, some of Wood’s ride out colleagues were stationed in the pumphouse. They monitored whether the Caterpillar pumps inside, four devices capable of handling 62,000 gallons of water per minute, could prevent the rising waters from overtaking the levee and flooding the manufacturing area a few hundred yards away near Building 320. But NASA protocol accounts for the safety of even its bravest ride out crew members. Once winds reach a certain gale force, everyone must be brought inside a secure area (in this case, Building 320) and remain on lockdown until the danger subsides. During Katrina, this tipping point came at 3am.
 “We don’t normally abandon the pumphouse, but we had to go get them in the middle of the night and bring them back,” Wood recalls. “So in the early morning, two guys took a dumptruck and it was pretty bleak—you couldn’t see the roadway, and it was dark on top of that. Katrina was about the first time I can remember in my years of being here that we lost electricity to the site. I mean the city lost electricity—that’s unique.“
 From that point on, Wood believes it was truly “touch and go.” Based on previous storms, he was confident the ride out team could get the facilities running again if only nature gave them the chance. But the destructive potential of Katrina was painfully evident even in the moment, and the ride out crew was well aware of the ramifications. This was 2005, the  Columbia tragedy had happened a mere two years earlier, and Michoud was expected to retrofit a number of external tanks as part of the mission to return to space. While everyone knew that the space program would be ending sometime in next the decade, losing Michoud would dramatically affect that timeframe.
 “If we lost the levee, we would shut down the NASA space program,” Wood says. “We manufacture every vehicle here, so how are you going to get to space unless you go through New Orleans? That’s the most catastrophic event you could’ve had. If Michoud was totally flooded, NASA has to say, ‘Alright, we’re out of the space business right now.’ That would’ve been years and years of damage.”
    Wood was the facilities director at the time, and as he saw it, drainage was never the issue. The facilities’ drainage system could hold a certain amount of water and, given some amount of time, that would eventually flow out. But if the pumps quit at all while the water was still coming, that calculation suddenly gets tragically out of balance.
 So that night, the team had to make a decision. It was possible to change the speed of the pumps, but they were water-cooled devices, and pushing them too hard ran the risk of overheating and failure. Ultimately, Wood and company chose to push the throttle—it worked out.
 “I never thought there’d be a risk, but the way it was raining, you could look at the roadways and know you were never going to pump that,” Wood says. “Our calculation was roughly a billion gallons of water swept out, so we kept the pumps going because you always had some kind of seepage coming back.”
 That next morning, the Michoud ride out team learned it had accomplished its primary task: the facility wasn’t underwater. However, it was seemingly the only thing on Old Gentilly Road—the main manufacturing drag of Michoud—that wasn’t.
 “We didn’t know until the next morning (8/30) that we were basically an island,” Wood says. “We were surrounded by water. During the night and that next morning, we knew there was a lot of rain and wind going on, but you never imagine you’ll be surrounded by water. We kept our pumps going and did the right things, what we were trained to do. That next day and the catastrophic 30 days later, that’s where you see people doing unusual things.”

到2016年，他的初创公司已经成长为一家年收入1000万美元的企业，在世界各地拥有100名员工。但当谈到在美国筹集资金时，海勒发现，硅谷外来者在没有人脉的情况下要进入公司大门并不容易。
他坚持了下来，2018年从Ignition Partners获得了1100万美元的首轮融资，这让他得以扩大业务。但他仍然在想，如果在这个过程的早期与硅谷一家处于早期阶段的风险投资公司合作，他的资本和指导是否会做得更好。
最近，我们采访了海勒，了解他如何在几乎没有外部帮助的情况下从无到有地创建了一家公司，以及筹集这些资金是什么感觉。
海勒在中国期间，他学会了如何驾驭制造业版图，并通过帮助大品牌在中国制造商品，建立了一家不错的咨询业务。但他看到了做更多事情的机会，特别是帮助希望在中国制造产品的小企业，批量要比大企业通常需要的小得多。
后者要困难得多，海勒感觉到可能会有商机，可以与Shopify等平台授权的小公司合作，以一种在线销售商品的方式。他们所缺乏的是一种制造它们的方法。
“我只是觉得，我们通过Shopify建立网络商店，并使用Instagram将信息传播出去的能力已经民主化，这简直是疯了。”对于这些小品牌来说，一切都已经民主化了，但制造环节仍然很难渗透。“海勒告诉TechCrunch。
他决定以这一想法为基础，创建一家公司，让小企业更容易从中国的微型工厂订购定制商品，让他们获得与大品牌相同的机会，但批量要小得多。这个想法变成了工作室。
“我们最终基本上与中国的这些小型微型工厂建立了关系，我们对这些工厂进行了培训，以便进行较小规模的批量制造，然后我们开发了软件，使这些中小企业能够向这些工厂下订单。因此，他们不再需要订购三万件，而是可以订购一百件。“
当海勒去寻找资金时，他已经将公司的年收入提高到了1000万美元，他相信自己有一个足够稳固的组织来吸引风险资本家的注意。
毕竟，这是他在该领域多年的经验基础上，精心打造并成长为一家健康的早期公司的企业。他已经把它带到市场上去了。他已经证明了产品与市场的契合性。他有客户。看起来这将是获得资金的重头戏。
然而，在现实中，他很难得到会面的机会。虽然身为黑人的海勒表示，黑人创始人很难进入风险投资公司，但他认为这是一个更大的问题的一部分，即那些没有正确关系的人普遍缺乏进入公司的渠道。
“首先，有些人根本无法接触到风投。而且这不仅仅是一个黑人问题。我认为这更多的是一个风投公司非常排外的问题，而且大多数白人都有这种类型的关系，“他说，”我认为这是一个风投公司非常排外的问题，往往是白人拥有这些类型的关系。“。
他补充说，“如果你不在硅谷，也不是那个非常专属的风险投资俱乐部，基本上就不可能筹集到资金，所以(在早期)这对我们来说从来都不是一个选择。”相反，他用自己的钱创办了公司，但当他把公司建设到他拥有的水平时，他想要外部资本，他相信自己处于一个很好的位置来获得这些资金。
海勒曾是一名风险投资家，他在加州大学伯克利分校(University of California，Berkeley)的时候，通过一种联系，得以获得一次会面。这导致了其他会议，这大多导致了很多失望。公平地说，任何人都很难闯入这个系统并提出一个令人信服的案例，但海勒已经将他的业务建立到了1000万美元的收入。这肯定是有价值的。
“很明显，我是硅谷的局外人，试图打入其中，而这已经是一项价值1000万美元的业务，拥有一支非常称职的工程团队。我们已经证明了很多事情，我觉得如果我是那种专属风投网络的一部分，我们筹集资金的速度会快得多，“海勒哀叹道。
他确实注意到，他认为身为黑人至少是他争取风投公司关注的一个因素。“对于非裔美国人和其他创业者来说，仅仅获得创业所需的初始资金尤其困难。我花了很多个人的钱，多年来都在犯错，因为我离资本中心太远了，“他说。
海勒说，他觉得自己可能因此而失去了一些东西。他说：“我见过无数拥有良好风投关系的创始人能够筹集到100万至500万美元的种子期资金，实际上没有产品，只有一个想法，”他说，“我见过无数拥有良好风投关系的创始人能够筹集100万至500万美元的种子轮，实际上没有产品，只有一个想法。”“这个选项对我来说是不可用的。”
经过18个月的会面，他终于从Ignition Partners获得了1100万美元。他说，由于他的奋斗以及他花费了时间和精力不断投球，当Ignition最终为他的公司提供资金时，这是一种巨大的成就感。
“这是我真正想要的，这在某种程度上证明了我们确实有一家真正的企业值得投资，”他说。
尽管海勒表示，由于流感大流行，今年国际制造业遇到了困难，但自2018年获得A轮融资以来，他的企业年收入已达2000万美元，员工约150人。
今年早些时候，他还推出了一项名为SuppliedShop.com的新业务，允许非常小的企业从工厂购买现成库存。他报告说，新业务已经在逐月增长50%。
人脉当然像海勒发现的那样重要，但有时也需要勇气、决心和一个好主意来创建一家公司。这就是海勒给这个过程带来的。他仍然认为，最好的办法是看结果，而不是专注于他达到目标所付出的斗争。
他说：“我确实认为，尽管存在种族主义和这些真正的斗争，但我也认为人们应该因为试图做出改变而得到认可，希望这将成为人们做出更多改变的催化剂。”

By 2016 he had grown his startup into a $10 million annual business with 100 employees around the world. But when it came to fundraising back in the U.S., Heller found it wasn’t easy for a Silicon Valley outsider to get in the door without connections.
 He persevered and in 2018 landed an $11 million Series A from Ignition Partners, which allowed him to expand his business. But he still wondered if he would have done even better with the capital and guidance that comes from working with an early-stage VC firm in Silicon Valley much earlier in the process.
  We sat down with Heller recently to learn how he built a company from the ground up with little outside help and what it was like to raise those funds.
  While Heller was in China, he learned how to navigate the manufacturing landscape and was able to build up a nice consulting business by helping big brands get goods manufactured there. But he saw an opportunity to do more, and especially to help smaller businesses looking to manufacture goods in China in much smaller batches than the big operations would typically require.
 The latter was much more difficult to do, and Heller sensed there could be a business opportunity to work with small companies empowered by platforms like Shopify with a way to sell goods online. What they lacked was a way to manufacture them.
 “I just felt that it’s crazy that we’ve democratized the ability to set up a web store with Shopify, and use Instagram to get the message out there. Everything’s been democratized for these small brands, but the manufacturing piece was still really hard to penetrate,” Heller told TechCrunch.
 He decided to build on that idea by creating a company that would make it easier for small businesses to order custom goods from micro factories in China, giving them access to the same opportunities as big brands, but in much smaller batches. That idea became The Studio.
 “We basically ended up building relationships with these small micro factories in China that we trained to run smaller batch manufacturing, and then we built software that enabled these SMBs to place orders with these factories. So instead of having to order 30,000 pieces, they can order 100 pieces,” he explained.
   When Heller went looking for funding, he had built the business to $10 million in annual revenue, and he believed that he had a solid enough organization to draw the attention of venture capitalists.
 After all, this was a business he had painstakingly built and grown into a healthy early-stage company based on years of experience in the field. He had taken it to market. He had proven product-market fit. He had customers. Seemed like it would be a slam dunk to get funding.
 In reality, though, he struggled to get meetings. While Heller, who is Black, says that it can be difficult for Black founders to get access to venture capital firms, he sees it as part of a larger issue of general lack of access for those who don’t have the right connections.
 “For starters, there are certain people that just don’t have access to VCs. And it’s not just a Black issue. I think it’s more of an issue of VCs just being very exclusive and it tends to be mostly White people that have those types of connections,” he said.
 He added, “If you’re not in Silicon Valley and not in that very exclusive VC club, it’s basically almost impossible to raise money and so that was never even an option for us [early on],” he said. Instead he bootstrapped the company with his own money, but when he had built the company to the level he had, he wanted outside capital, and he believed he was in a good position to get it.
  Heller was able to get a meeting through a connection from his days at the University of California, Berkeley, who had been a venture capitalist. That led to other meetings, which led mostly to a lot of disappointment. To be fair, it’s hard for anyone to break into this system and present a compelling case, but Heller had built his business to $10 million in revenue. That had to count for something.
 “It was very clear that I was an outsider in Silicon Valley trying to penetrate it, and this was already a $10 million business with a very competent engineering team. We had proven out a lot of things, and I feel that if I were part of that kind of exclusive VC network, we would have raised money a lot quicker,” Heller lamented.
 He did note that he believed being Black was at least a factor in his struggle to get attention from VC firms. “It is particularly difficult for African American and other founders to just get initial capital to start their business. I spent a lot of my personal money, and years making mistakes, because I was so far away from the centers of capital,” he said.
 Heller says he felt he might have lost something along the way because of that.”I’ve seen countless founders that have good VC connections able to raise $1 million to $5 million seed rounds, with literally no product and just an idea,” he said. “This option was not available to me.”
  After 18 months of meetings, he finally received $11 million from Ignition Partners. He said because of his struggle and the time and energy he took to keep pitching, it was a great feeling of accomplishment when Ignition finally funded his company.
 “This was something that I really wanted, and it kind of validated that we did have a real business that was worthy of being funded,” he said.
 Although Heller says this year has been difficult for international manufacturing due to the pandemic, he has built his business to $20 million in annual revenue and around 150 employees since getting his A round in 2018.
 He also launched a new business earlier this year called  SuppliedShop.com, which allows very small businesses to buy ready-made inventory from factories. He reports that the new business is already growing 50% month over month.
 Connections certainly count as Heller found, but sometimes it also takes grit and determination and a good idea to build a company. That’s what Heller brought to this process. He still believes that it’s best to look at the outcome, rather than focus on the struggle it took him to get there.
 “I do think that, although there is racism and there are these real struggles, I also think people should be recognized for trying to make changes, and hopefully this will be a catalyst for people making more change,” he said.
title: 为什么一家年收入1000万美元的初创公司花了18个月的时间才获得风险投资
title_en: Why a startup with $10M in annual revenue took 18 months to get VC funding

note:
这家英国初创公司是众多应用人工智能自动化工作的公司之一，否则这些工作将由法律专业人员完成，目的是提高运营效率。其他进入这一领域的初创公司包括Kira Systems、LawGeex和Lignance等。
ThoughtRiver辩称，它与大多数合同视图公司的重点不同，因为它专注于预签署合同-目的是让交易更快达成。“几乎所有其他人都只是受雇从现有合同中提取数据。销售团队对ThoughtRiver的需求与法律对它的需求一样多，“一位发言人告诉我们。
这笔首轮投资是在这家成立于2015年的初创公司经历了12个月的显著增长之后进行的，该公司表示，其自动合同审查软件现在正被G4S、Singtel和DB Schenker等公司使用。据这位发言人称，该公司于2017年底推出了一项服务，目前在全球拥有超过25名客户。
它还大肆宣扬与专业服务公司普华永道(PwC)签署战略合作伙伴关系-根据一份新闻稿，普华永道将为其客户开发一项由ThoughtRiver软件支持的服务。
与由内部律师进行的初步合同审查相比，ThoughtRiver吹捧高达95%的时间和80%的成本节约。该公司的首要主张是“更快的合同审查速度意味着交易流动速度的提高”。
在技术方面，ThoughtRiver创建了一个合同法律逻辑本体，将其表述为一系列详细的问题，与其自然语言处理(NLP)引擎相结合，使其软件能够通过生成风险评估来预先筛选合同。它还将建议对法律术语进行调整，以补救问题，包括通过Microsoft Word插件，客户的内部律师可能更喜欢在那里工作。
这家初创公司吹捧的其他好处包括数据提取，以支持大规模的合同分析-例如尽职调查或评估监管变化的影响。它的销售宣传还表明，轻松获取合同职位概述通过建立更知情的业务关系来帮助客户。
ThoughtRiver已经在纽约、新加坡、伦敦、剑桥和奥克兰设立了办事处。该公司表示，新资金将用于美国市场的进一步增长，并将加大在美国市场的销售和营销力度。扩大与主要技术合作伙伴的整合也是有可能的。
章鱼风险投资公司(Octopus Ventures)的早期投资者Akriti Dokania在一份声明中评论这笔融资时表示：“虽然与其他行业相比，法律界采用人工智能的速度一直较慢，但ThoughtRiver拥有一种行之有效的商业模式，其基础是为律师解决一个根本问题。”通过使用高级自然语言处理引擎来加快合同审查速度、加快交易流程和业务增长速度，法律专业人员可以比以往任何时候都更高效地工作。我们很高兴能够支持ThoughtRiver团队的全球扩张计划，因为该公司颠覆了现有的市场和一整套流程。“
note_en:
The UK startup is one of a number applying AI to automate work that would otherwise be done by legal professions with the aim of boosting operational efficiency. Other startups playing in the space include the likes of  Kira Systems,  LawGeex and  Luminance to name a few.
 ThoughtRiver   argues it has a different focus vs the majority of contract view companies because it’s focusing on pre-signature contracts — with the aim of making securing a deal faster. “Almost all others are just employed to pull data from existing contracts. ThoughtRiver is as much in demand by Sales teams as it is by Legal,” a spokesman told us.
  The Series A investment comes after twelve month’s of what it’s billed as significant growth for the 2015-founded startup, which says its automated contract review software is now being used by the likes of G4S, Singtel and DB Schenker. It launched a service at the end of 2017 and now has more than 25 customers around the world, per the spokesman.
 It also trumpets inking a strategic partnership with professional services firm PwC — which will see the latter developing a service for its clients powered by ThoughtRiver’s software, according to a press release.
 ThoughtRiver touts up to 95% in time and 80% in cost savings vs an initial contract review that’s carried out by in-house lawyers. And ‘faster contract reviews sum to increased deal flow velocity’ is its overarching claim.
 On the tech side, ThoughtRiver has created an ontology of contract legal logic, couched as a series of detailed questions which, combined with its natural language processing (NLP) engine, enables its software to pre-screen contracts by generating a risk assessment. It will also suggest tweaks to the legalese to remediate problems, including via a plug-in for Microsoft Word, where customers’ in-house lawyers may prefer to work.
 Other benefits the startup touts are data extraction to power contract analytics at scale — such as for due diligence or to assess the impact of regulatory change. Its sale pitch also suggests that easy access to an overview of contractual positions helps customers by enabling better-informed business relationships.
  ThoughtRiver has already established offices in New York, Singapore, London, Cambridge and Auckland. It says the new funding will be put towards further growth in the US market, where it will be dialling up sales and marketing efforts. Expanding integrations with major tech partners is also on the cards.
 Commenting on the funding in a statement, Akriti Dokania, early stage investor at Octopus Ventures, said: “While the legal sector has been slow to adopt AI compared to other industries, ThoughtRiver has a proven business model based on solving a fundamental issue for lawyers. By using an advanced Natural Language Processing engine to drive faster contract reviews and acceleration of deal flow and business growth, legal professionals can work more efficiently than ever. We are thrilled to support the ThoughtRiver team with its plans for global expansion as the firm disrupts an established market and set of processes.”
title: ThoughtRiver净赚1000万美元，通过人工智能合同审查加快交易速度
title_en: ThoughtRiver nabs $10M to speed up deal-making with AI contract review
